planting.note: Direct sow in spring or late summer; rat-tail radish tolerates heat better than rooting radishes and is grown for its crisp, peppery seed pods rather than a root. In zone 9 plant in March–April for spring harvest or August–September for fall. It bolts quickly and intentionally — bolting is the goal.

directions: Direct sow 1/4 inch deep in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; unlike rooting radishes, rat-tail radish is allowed to bolt and flower because the edible product is the long, curved seed pods that develop after flowering. The pods, harvested young at 3–6 inches long, are crisp and peppery — eaten raw, pickled, or stir-fried in Indian (mooli ki phali) and Southeast Asian cuisines. The plant grows tall and needs no support despite its airy habit; the attractive white to lilac flowers are also edible and attract pollinators. Pick pods while still tender and bright green, before they develop fibrous walls; daily harvest encourages continued pod set.
sources: Purdue University NewCROP — Raphanus sativus Caudatus Group
University of California Cooperative Extension — Specialty Radishes
Cornell University Vegetable Breeding Institute — Raphanus

planting.note: Direct sow on soil surface (seeds need light to germinate) after last frost in warm soil; in zone 9 plant March through June. Thin to 12–18 inches as seedlings crowd. Grain amaranth grows rapidly and thrives in hot, dry summers, producing massive seed heads that hang heavy with tiny grain.

directions: Scatter seeds on the surface of well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun — do not bury them more than 1/8 inch or germination fails. Grain amaranth is drought-tolerant once established and outperforms most grains in summer heat; it fixes nitrogen through a C4 photosynthesis pathway that is highly efficient at high temperatures. Harvest when the seed head feels dry and seeds begin to fall when brushed — cut the entire head into a paper bag and allow to finish drying for 1–2 weeks before threshing. The tiny seeds are cooked like quinoa, popped like miniature popcorn, or ground into flour; they are nutritionally exceptional, with all essential amino acids and high calcium content. Young leaves are also edible and nutritious as cooked greens.
sources: USDA Sustainable Agriculture Research and Education — Grain Amaranth
University of Wisconsin Extension — Grain Amaranth as a Cover Crop

planting.note: Surface sow after last frost in warm soil; in zone 9 direct sow from March through August for a continuous supply of summer greens. Vegetable amaranth grows explosively in heat and humidity and is ready for first harvest in as little as 3 weeks after germination.

directions: Scatter seeds on the surface of fertile, moist soil in full sun; thin or transplant to final spacing, using thinnings as baby greens. Vegetable amaranth thrives in conditions that defeat spinach, lettuce, and other cool-season greens, making it the premier summer leafy vegetable for hot, humid climates. Harvest entire young plants thinly, or cut growing tips regularly from larger plants to stimulate bushy regrowth; the tricolor varieties also produce striking ornamental foliage in red, yellow, and green. Young leaves and tips are cooked as a spinach substitute in Asian, Caribbean, and West African cuisines — they cook down dramatically and benefit from high heat in a wok. Remove plants before seed heads shatter if naturalizing is not desired.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Amaranth as a Vegetable
Asian Vegetable Research and Development Center — Amaranth

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost once soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March through June. Sorghum-sudangrass hybrids can be planted through July for late-summer cover. Sorghum is one of the most drought- and heat-tolerant of all summer crops, making it ideal for the Texas and Gulf Coast heat.

directions: Drill or broadcast seed 1–1.5 inches deep in a wide range of soils including heavy clays and infertile ground; sorghum establishes a deep root system that loosens compacted subsoil and mines deeply-held nutrients. As a summer cover crop it suppresses weeds aggressively and builds organic matter; mow or disk before seed heads shatter to prevent volunteers. As a grain crop the seed heads can be harvested for bird feed, home brewing (sorghum is the base grain for many African and Asian fermented beverages), or ground into gluten-free flour. The juice squeezed from sweet sorghum stalks is boiled down to sorghum molasses, a traditional Southern sweetener. Beware allelopathic compounds released by sorghum residues — delay direct-seeding small-seeded crops for 3–4 weeks after incorporation.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Grain Sorghum
USDA SARE — Managing Cover Crops Profitably: Sorghum-Sudangrass
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Grain Sorghum Production

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring as new growth emerges, or plant nursery transplants. In zone 9 Japanese painted fern grows year-round as a semi-evergreen to deciduous perennial, going partially dormant in summer heat; the fronds emerge brilliantly silvered in spring and early fall.

directions: Plant in rich, consistently moist, well-drained soil in deep to partial shade; Japanese painted fern is prized for its silver-marked, wine-red-stemmed fronds that illuminate dark corners of shaded beds and woodland gardens. It grows best under deciduous trees where it receives winter light and spring moisture before the canopy fills in. In zone 9 it performs most vigorously in the cooler months from October through May; the fronds may scorch and retreat in July–August heat but typically recover with resumed growth in fall. Divide clumps every 3–4 years to rejuvenate and propagate; the species has yielded numerous cultivars including 'Pictum' (the most common silver-painted form) and 'Ursula's Red'.
sources: Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Athyrium niponicum
American Fern Society — Growing Ferns in the Garden
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Ferns in the Landscape
